title: Seven Kinds of People You Find in Bookshops

chapters.title: Inside the bookstore world
chapters.text:
Shaun Bythell runs the largest second-hand bookshop in Scotland, and his love for the trade is matched by his worry about where it is heading. After more than twenty years behind the counter, he started sorting his customers into a Linnaean-style system with Latin names. He calls them Peritus, Familia Juvenis, Homo qui maleficas amat, Homo qui desidet, Senex cum barba, Viator non tacitus, and Parentum historiae studiosus.

A bookshop only works when the seller learns what each customer wants and adapts to them.

Bythell also added a bonus group for the Staff and their many types, and he described the dream customer that every seller would love to meet.

Read on and you will meet the sly Antiquarian, the shy Erotica Browser, the clueless Student Hugo, the eager Sci-Fi Fan, the hungry Railway Collector, and the rare Perfect Customer.

chapters.title: Genus: The self-appointed expert
chapters.text:
The Expert has no real audience, yet they speak as if a crowd waits for their wisdom. They reach for the longest, most complex word available when a short one would do, mostly to feel clever.

Five species fall under this genus.

First, the Specialist. A bookshop covers so many subjects that no seller can know them all, and Specialists love that gap. They walk in to lecture the seller about their narrow field and enjoy the fact that the seller cannot argue back. Some are polite enough to notice that their passion is not universal, but they still take pride in being different.

Second, the Bore. The Bore shares an opinion on every topic, and sometimes jumps into other customers' conversations with rude rants. They call themselves a polymath. The best move is to avoid them or stay silent, because any small sign of interest triggers an endless speech on the most random subjects.

Third, the Helpful Person. Some experts are a gift. They drop a few sharp facts into a short chat and leave the seller wanting more. Their tidbits arm the seller with the knowledge they need to sound confident in front of the next customer.

Fourth, the Antiquarian Book Collector. They care about the book as a physical thing, not for the words inside. Their skill is spotting the small details that set one edition apart from another, and they always know the mistakes that mark a particular printing. They will grumble about prices and swear they saw the same copy cheaper somewhere else.

Fifth, the Home Mechanic. The Home Mechanic thinks every book should be about cars. They hunt for manuals and even pick up copies for friends, then use what they read to fix real problems.

Of the five, the Specialist and the Bore are the two you wish would leave.

chapters.title: Genus: The young family
chapters.text:
Young families bring their kids into the shop and quietly undo all the work the seller did to keep the place tidy. The genus has four species worth knowing.

The first is the Exhausted Parents. They are usually a couple with a large family, often with at least one baby. Their real reason for visiting is to tire the children out so they can finally get some quiet.

The second is the Abandoned Child. It is common to see a father walk in with his child, hand them a picture book, and steer them toward the kids' corner. Then he slips toward the door and tells the seller he will be right back. The promised minute usually stretches into much more, and the return comes with weak excuses and fake apologies.

The third is the Aspirational Parents. They want their children to read well, so they push books on them that the kids do not care about. This pressure tends to make the children unhappy and slows their reading growth. Children who pick their own books tend to read more and enjoy it more.

The fourth is the Book-loving Children. These young readers love books and often drag a reluctant parent to the shop with them.

When you design the children's section, keep adults in mind, because many of them come back for the books they loved as kids.

chapters.title: Genus: Occultist
chapters.text:
The Occultist is sure that the unbelievable is real, and that certainty gives them a strange sense of being above everyone else. They usually browse alone, and their social skills and personal hygiene are not always great.

There are five types in this genus.

The first is the Dark Artist. Dressed in black, often carrying extra weight, and drawn to the works of Aleister Crowley, they tend to be men who see occult book sellers as unbelievers. They treat the sale of mystical books as a betrayal of the deep secrets they follow, and the group also includes some Wiccans.

The second is the Conspiracy Theorist. This quiet group asks about strange subjects, from the events of September 11 to the Kennedy assassination to antisemitic plots. They blend in at first, so they are easy to miss. When they come in to sell books, the bags usually hold titles on UFOs, voodoo, and poltergeists that few others want. Respect their interests, because they are loyal to unusual theories.

The third is the Tarot Reader. They gaze into crystal balls to predict the future, and their wider practice can include dream reading, Celtic mythology, horoscopes, palm reading, spiritual healing, and rune casting. They tend to wear loose clothes covered in pet hair and leave the smell of old incense behind them. Convinced that people will pay for their gifts, they often buy self-help books to set up a small business.

The fourth is the Ghost Hunter. Most people say they believe in ghosts, but very few bookshop customers actually hunt for ghost books. The few who do leave a strong, sometimes annoying, impression.

The fifth is the Craft Enthusiast. Their look is so close to the Tarot Reader that the two are easy to confuse. Mostly women, they drift through the shop with a mysterious air, never quite sure what they want, and they often leave without buying anything useful.

For context, a 2021 Ipsos poll found that 36 percent of Americans believe in ghosts and 39 percent believe in UFOs.

chapters.title: Genus: Loiterer
chapters.text:
The Loiterer shows up for no clear reason and hovers in a way that feels slightly off. They give off the feeling that they are about to ask a question, so the seller stays close. When they finally speak, the question is never about books. They want the time, a good lunch place, or the nearest bus stop.

Loiterers make the air feel heavier than it is.

There are four species in this genus.

The first is the Erotica Browser. They are usually older men, dressed to avoid notice in long coats and hats, sometimes with dark glasses. They try to look casual as they slide adult books into other sections, like the railway shelves, and they swap dust jackets without putting them back. Giggling teenagers also fall into this group, wandering the aisles and laughing at the racier titles until they hear a parent and scatter. A sharp contrast is the young adult woman who reads the same shelves calmly, with mixed feelings and no rush.

The second is the Loiterer without Intent. They come in to kill time while waiting on something nearby, like a takeout order or a car wash. They have no plan to buy, and they flip through pages just to fill the wait.

The third is the Bored Spouse. They find books dull and look for a soft corner to settle in, often playing games on their phone while their partner browses. Crossed arms, loud sighs, and a constant watch check are the usual signs that they have had enough.

The fourth is the Self-published Author. Self-publishing has opened the door to everyone from talented writers to weaker ones. Without a big publisher behind them, these authors often approach sellers and try to convince them to stock their books.

chapters.title: Genus: Bearded pensioner
chapters.text:
Most members of this genus tour the country in a motorhome or caravan, complain about everything, and never buy a thing. They may park their vehicle right in front of the shop and never worry that they are blocking the view.

There are four species in this genus.

The first is the Lycra-clad. Usually a couple, they walk straight to the map section, spread out every chart to plan their week, and end up blocking a doorway. They refold the maps wrong and leave without a word to the staff.

The second is the Pantalons Rouges. Mostly older and either large or tall and thin, they are not browsers. They know what they want and walk right to it with a steady focus. Price is not a concern.

The third is the Downsizers. They are only seen in second-hand shops. Usually pensioners, they try to convince the seller that the book they want to sell is worth a fortune. In most cases, they are moving to a smaller home and want to let go of a few things. Be kind to them, because the items they bring often mean a lot.

The fourth is the Miser. Misers are stingy people who cannot accept that prices rise. Some get loud and accuse the seller of overcharging.

chapters.title: Genus: The Not-So-Silent Traveler
chapters.text:
The Silent Traveler was the pen name of Chang Yee, a writer who looked at everyday life with curious wonder. His skill came from seeing things as an outsider without all the local context. By watching quietly, he caught the beauty in the ordinary and shared it with his readers in a fresh way.

This genus has five species.

The first is the Whistler. They are not there to buy. They wander the shop and whistle, and the tune is rarely musical. The stares do not make them stop.

The second is the Sniffer. They have a cold and refuse to blow their nose, so they sniff loudly as they wander the shelves and pick up books.

The third is the Hummer. Where the Whistler makes random noise, the Hummer tries to stay close to a real tune. When both the Whistler and the Hummer visit the same shop on the same day, the noise is hard to bear.

The fourth is the Farter. Whether the result is silent or loud, they usually find a quiet corner for the deed, though sometimes the counter is the spot they choose. Spotting them in time is not always easy.

The fifth is the Tutter. They are easy to spot by the constant head shake and the unhappy sounds they make. Nothing the seller does seems good enough, and the only thing they enjoy is finding fault.

The Not-So-Silent Traveler does not watch quietly. They like attention, on purpose or not.

chapters.title: Genus: Family Historian
chapters.text:
Bythell believes that tracing a family line is not as special as people think, since we all share the same distant roots.

He has also said that people who study their family tree are usually trying to prove a small point.

Many Americans with roots in rural Scotland still feel a strong bond to their clan. They walk into bookshops hoping to find books about their family, and they often expect there to be far more books on their particular line than there really are. Bythell sees this as a mostly American habit, although a strong interest in family roots can show up in other countries, including Australia and New Zealand.

Tracing a family line through the generations can give a person a stronger sense of identity.

chapters.title: Genus: Staff
chapters.text:
The staff is the bridge between the books on the shelves and the people who come in to buy them. They are the face of the shop, and they keep the whole thing running.

There are five types of staff.

The first is Student Hugo. He is usually a relative of the owner, comes from a wealthy family, and is in the shop because a bored parent or a playful godparent thought a summer behind the counter would do him good. Hugo is not strong in the role, struggles to get along with other staff, and rarely helps customers well.

The second is Student Mary. Like Hugo, she works through a university break, but her reasons are different. She needs the money and has a love of art, which makes her more able to help. She still fights with self-doubt, even though she is useful.

The third is the Hipster. The Hipster is the opposite of Mary. They are in the shop to be seen, not to help. They give off a distant air, lost in their own thoughts or a book, often stroking their beard. When a customer interrupts, their response ranges from unhelpful to annoyed.

The fourth is the Second-hand Bookseller. Usually self-employed, they sell books because they have to, not because they chose to. They prefer paper over digital, and years at the counter have given them a visible dislike for customers. They have been in the trade so long that they cannot remember exactly how they started.

The fifth is the Manager. Well dressed, on time, and full of energy, the Manager believes the customer is always right. They are rare in second-hand shops and far more common in stores that sell new books, which sets them apart from the earlier three types.

If you really need help in the shop, look for a Manager or a Student Mary.

chapters.title: Genus: Perfect customer
chapters.text:
The customers a bookshop owner dreams about love being surrounded by second-hand books, and they value the worlds that clever authors have built. For them, reading is a way to step away from the everyday. This kind of customer is rare now, because the rise of e-books means fewer people care about paper copies.

Any edition can turn out to be a rare treasure years later, so it pays to treat books with care.

One type of perfect customer is the fiction collector, who is always on the hunt for rare editions and never haggles. Another is the railway collector, who treats the seller like royalty and reads anything about trains. A third is the reading enthusiast who walks in without a title in mind but leaves with a book. They are open to suggestions, and they happily pay the asking price without a fight. The last is the Sci-Fi Fan, who never comes alone. They know the other fans and spread the word about new collections. They are rarely disappointed, as long as the shop stocks their favorite authors.

George R.R. Martin once said that a reader lives a thousand lives before they die, while the person who never reads only lives one.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Booksellers deal with every kind of customer each day, from the rude to the charming. Beyond the money from sales, the small interactions with each quirky buyer are what make the job interesting. No matter how harsh a customer seems on the surface, there is usually a kind person underneath.

The book trade is more than a simple buyer and seller. Knowing the different types of customers forces the seller to step into other roles at different times, including detective, psychologist, agent, or scholar. Whichever role is needed, it always pulls the seller a little further from the basic job.

When sorting book lovers into types, remember that any system only captures broad traits. People are far more complex, and each one carries a mix of qualities that no label can fully cover.

Try this. From the broad list of customers and staff in a bookshop, find the type you fit into. Think about how you could be a better customer or a better staff member.
